section.title: The Shrewd Manager and the Rich Man and Lazarus
section.sectionRationale: Jesus tells of a manager about to be fired who shrewdly uses his master's wealth to secure his future, and He turns the story into a summons to use money now for eternal ends, for no one can serve both God and money. The money-loving Pharisees scoff, so Jesus warns them with the account of a rich man who feasted while Lazarus starved at his gate, and who woke in torment across an unbridgeable chasm. Those who will not hear Moses and the Prophets will not be persuaded even by one who rises from the dead.
section.thesis: Money is a servant to be spent for eternity or a master that damns, so Jesus calls His people to shrewd, faithful, generous stewardship in light of the world to come, and warns that a heart deaf to Scripture will not be changed even by a resurrection.
section.frameNote: Three movements: the parable of the shrewd manager and its lessons on money; the scoffing Pharisees, the law, and the kingdom; and the rich man and Lazarus, the great chasm, and the sufficiency of Moses and the Prophets.

units.label: The shrewd manager
units.note: use worldly wealth to make friends for eternity

verses.text: Jesus also said to His disciples, “There was a rich man whose manager was accused of wasting his possessions.
So he called him in to ask, ‘What is this I hear about you? Turn in an account of your management, for you cannot be manager any longer.’
The manager said to himself, ‘What shall I do, now that my master is taking away my position? I am too weak to dig and too ashamed to beg.
I know what I will do so that after my removal from management, people will welcome me into their homes.’
And he called in each one of his master’s debtors. ‘How much do you owe my master?’ he asked the first.
‘A hundred measures of olive oil,’ he answered. ‘Take your bill,’ said the manager, ‘sit down quickly, and write fifty.’
Then he asked another, ‘And how much do you owe?’ ‘A hundred measures of wheat,’ he replied. ‘Take your bill and write eighty,’ he told him.
The master commended the dishonest manager because he had acted {{phronimos|shrewdly}}. For the sons of this age are more shrewd in dealing with their own kind than are the sons of light.
I tell you, use worldly wealth to make friends for yourselves so that when it is gone, they will welcome you into eternal dwellings.

units.label: Faithful with money, and two masters
units.note: you cannot serve both God and money

verses.text: Whoever is {{pistos|faithful}} with very little will also be faithful with much, and whoever is dishonest with very little will also be dishonest with much.
So if you have not been faithful with worldly wealth, who will entrust you with true riches?
And if you have not been faithful with the belongings of another, who will give you belongings of your own?
No servant can serve two masters. Either he will hate the one and love the other, or he will be devoted to the one and despise the other. You cannot serve both God and {{mamonas|money}}.”

units.label: The scoffing Pharisees, the law, and the kingdom
units.note: what is prized among men is detestable before God

verses.text: The Pharisees, who were lovers of money, heard all of this and were scoffing at Jesus.
So He said to them, “You are the ones who justify yourselves before men, but God knows your hearts. For what is prized among men is detestable before God.
The Law and the Prophets were proclaimed until John. Since that time, the gospel of the kingdom of God is being preached, and everyone is forcing his way into it.
But it is easier for heaven and earth to pass away than for a single stroke of a pen to drop out of the Law.
Anyone who divorces his wife and marries another woman commits adultery, and he who marries a divorced woman commits adultery.

units.label: The rich man and Lazarus
units.note: they have Moses and the Prophets

verses.text: Now there was a rich man dressed in purple and fine linen, who lived each day in joyous splendor.
And a beggar named Lazarus lay at his gate, covered with sores
and longing to be fed with the crumbs that fell from the rich man’s table. Even the dogs came and licked his sores.
One day the beggar died and was carried by the angels to Abraham’s side. And the rich man also died and was buried.
In Hades, where he was in torment, he looked up and saw Abraham from afar, with Lazarus by his side.
So he cried out, ‘Father Abraham, have mercy on me and send Lazarus to dip the tip of his finger in water and cool my tongue. For I am in agony in this fire.’
But Abraham answered, ‘Child, remember that during your lifetime you received your good things, while Lazarus received bad things. But now he is comforted here, while you are in agony.
And besides all this, a great {{chasma|chasm}} has been fixed between us and you, so that even those who wish cannot cross from here to you, nor can anyone cross from there to us.’
‘Then I beg you, father,’ he said, ‘send Lazarus to my father’s house,
for I have five brothers. Let him warn them, so that they will not also end up in this place of torment.’
But Abraham replied, ‘They have Moses and the Prophets; let your brothers listen to them.’
‘No, father Abraham,’ he said, ‘but if someone is sent to them from the dead, they will repent.’
Then Abraham said to him, ‘If they do not listen to Moses and the Prophets, they will not be persuaded even if someone rises from the dead.’”

phronimos.gloss: Prudent, shrewd, sensible. The master commends the dishonest manager not for his dishonesty but for his foresight: facing a crisis, he acted decisively to secure his future. Jesus wishes the sons of light were as clear-eyed and urgent about eternity as the sons of this age are about money.

pistos.gloss: Faithful, trustworthy. How a person handles money, a very little thing, reveals whether he can be trusted with true and lasting riches. Faithfulness in small, worldly stewardship is the training ground and the test for greater things God would entrust.

mamonas.gloss: Mammon, money or wealth personified as a rival master. Jesus does not merely say wealth is dangerous; He makes it a would-be lord that competes with God for the heart's devotion. One cannot be a slave to both; money must be servant or it becomes god.

chasma.gloss: A chasm, a gaping gulf. Between the comforted Lazarus and the tormented rich man a great gulf has been fixed, uncrossable in either direction. The word underlines the finality of the divide beyond death, and the urgency of hearing God's word now, while the gate of mercy still stands open.

basic.context: Jesus tells His disciples of a manager caught wasting his master's goods and about to be fired. Before the axe falls, he shrewdly reduces what his master's debtors owe, so they will take him in later. The master commends his foresight, and Jesus draws the lesson: use money now for eternal ends, be faithful with it, and remember that you cannot serve both God and money. The Pharisees, who loved money, scoff. So Jesus tells of a rich man who feasted daily while a beggar named Lazarus starved at his gate. Both die: Lazarus is carried to Abraham's side, the rich man wakes in torment. A great chasm now divides them. The rich man begs that his brothers be warned, but Abraham says they have Moses and the Prophets; if they will not hear them, they will not believe even a resurrection.
basic.hermeneutics: Read the chapter as teaching on money in the light of eternity. The parable is not praising dishonesty but urgency and foresight. The sayings apply it: be faithful, choose your master. The Lazarus story then dramatizes the eternal stakes and warns that a heart deaf to Scripture will not be moved even by a miracle.
basic.originalLanguages: The manager acted phronimos, shrewdly, with foresight about his future. Disciples are called to be pistos, faithful, with money, a very little thing. No one can serve both God and mamonas, money treated as a rival master. And beyond death a great chasma, an uncrossable gulf, is fixed between comfort and torment.
basic.typology: The rich man's ignored gate recalls the covenant call to care for the poor at your doors. Abraham's side pictures the gathering of the faithful to their father in the age to come. And the closing line about one rising from the dead points ahead to Jesus' own resurrection, which a hardened generation would still refuse.
basic.christ: Jesus is the true and faithful Steward who spends everything, even His life, for others, and the poor man's advocate against the callous rich. He is the One who will rise from the dead, the sign the rich man's brothers would not need if they heard Moses and the Prophets, and the One to whom all the Scriptures point.
basic.god: God knows the heart behind the money-loving self-justification of men, and He counts as detestable what people prize. He receives the ignored beggar and comforts him, and He upholds a settled justice beyond death. He speaks sufficiently in His written word, so that those who will not hear it have no excuse.
basic.crossReferences: Matthew 6 gives the same saying, you cannot serve God and money. 1 Timothy 6 warns that the love of money is a root of all kinds of evil. Deuteronomy 15 commands open-handed care for the poor at your gate. John 11 shows a different Lazarus raised, yet many still would not believe.
basic.oneStory: As He nears Jerusalem, Jesus presses His people to hold money loosely and spend it for eternity, warning that it can become a master that damns. The rich man and Lazarus reveal the eternal weight of how we live now, and the closing word, they have Moses and the Prophets, points to the sufficiency of Scripture and to the risen Christ whom it all foretells.

context.literary: The chapter pairs two rich men, one shrewd and one complacent, framing teaching on wealth within Luke's travel narrative. It follows the parables of chapter 15 about the lost, and turns from grace received to grace lived out in the use of possessions. The scoffing Pharisees of verse 14 tie the two parables together as a rebuke to money-loving self-justification.
context.historical: A steward or manager handled a wealthy landowner's affairs and could adjust accounts, which is why the debtors obey him. Debts in oil and wheat were enormous, hundreds of gallons and tons. Purple and fine linen marked extravagant wealth; a beggar at the gate longing for scraps, with dogs licking his sores, was the picture of destitution. Abraham's bosom was a Jewish image for the blessed dead.
context.canonical: The care of the poor at one's gate is a covenant demand running through the law and prophets. The reversal of the rich and the poor echoes the Magnificat and the Beatitudes. The insistence that Moses and the Prophets are enough, and that resurrection will not persuade the willfully deaf, is confirmed tragically in the Gospels when the risen Christ is rejected.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To teach disciples to use money shrewdly and faithfully for eternal ends under the lordship of God rather than mammon, to expose the money-loving self-justification of the Pharisees, and to warn through Lazarus that eternity hangs on how we hear God's word and treat the poor now.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The parable of the manager and the story of Lazarus are illustrative narratives, not endorsements of the manager's fraud or reports of the geography of the afterlife. Their prescriptive force is clear: be faithful with wealth, serve God not money, care for the poor, and heed Moses and the Prophets while there is time.

debates.question: What exactly is commended in the shrewd manager?

views.label: His decisive foresight, not his dishonesty
views.case: The text calls him the dishonest manager even as it commends his shrewdness; what is praised is that, facing a reckoning, he acted urgently and cleverly to secure his future. Jesus laments that the sons of light lack this same clear-eyed urgency about eternity, and applies it to using money for lasting good.

views.label: A lightening of unjust debts or the steward's own commission
views.case: Some argue he was cancelling the interest or his own cut rather than defrauding the master, which would explain the master's approval on honest grounds. On this reading the manager repents of exploitation and acts justly, and is praised for genuine good, not mere cleverness.

debates.bearing: Either way the application is the same: money is fleeting and the reckoning is sure, so use worldly wealth now, decisively and generously, for friends and treasure that outlast it.

debates.question: How should the rich man and Lazarus be read?

views.label: A parable teaching the eternal stakes of how we live and hear
views.case: Like the surrounding parables it uses vivid, culturally familiar imagery to make a moral and spiritual point: wealth wrongly held damns, the ignored poor are precious to God, and Scripture is sufficient to warn. The details serve the message rather than mapping the afterlife precisely.

views.label: A window onto the reality of conscious existence after death
views.case: Whatever its parabolic form, Jesus affirms real states beyond death, comfort and torment, memory and longing, and a fixed, uncrossable divide. The story presses the sober truth that eternity is settled by this life and cannot be renegotiated later.

debates.bearing: Both hold together the urgency Jesus intends: this life matters eternally, the poor at our gate matter to God, and the time to hear His word and repent is now, before the chasm is fixed.

study.originalLanguages: The master commends the manager because he acted phronimos, shrewdly, with the foresight of a man who sees his crisis and moves at once, and Jesus wishes the sons of light were as urgent about eternity. Disciples are to be pistos, faithful, with money, which is a very little thing, since faithfulness in the small and worldly is the test for true riches. The heart of the teaching is that no one can serve both God and mamonas, mammon, wealth personified as a rival lord that demands the soul's devotion. And in the story of Lazarus a great chasma, a fixed and gaping gulf, separates comfort from torment beyond death, uncrossable in either direction, so that the word to hear is now, while the gate still stands open and Moses and the Prophets still speak.

typology.type: The poor at your gate whom the covenant commands you to care for, Deuteronomy 15:7-11
typology.fulfillment: Lazarus ignored at the rich man's gate, whose neglect brings the rich man to torment, Luke 16:20-25.

typology.type: The gathering of the faithful to Abraham their father
typology.fulfillment: Lazarus carried by angels to Abraham's side, the comfort of the blessed dead, Luke 16:22.

typology.type: One rising from the dead to warn the living
typology.fulfillment: Christ risen, whom those deaf to Scripture would still refuse, Luke 16:31.

study.christ: Jesus is the faithful Steward who, unlike the wasteful manager, is entrusted with everything and spends it all, pouring out even His life, for the good of others. He stands as the advocate of the ignored Lazarus and the judge of the callous rich, the One who knows the hearts that justify themselves before men. And He is the answer to the parable's last word: someone will rise from the dead, and yet those who will not hear Moses and the Prophets will refuse Him still, for the same Scriptures that they ignore point to Him from beginning to end. To hear the word is to find Christ; to refuse it is to refuse the risen Lord.
study.god: God sees past the respectable surface to the money-loving heart and declares that what men prize can be detestable in His sight. He is the God who receives the ignored beggar and carries him to comfort, and who holds a settled, unbendable justice beyond the grave. He has spoken clearly and sufficiently in Moses and the Prophets, so that no one who refuses His word can plead that He did not warn them. This is a God who cares fiercely for the poor at the gate and who cannot be served alongside the idol of wealth.

crossReferences.note: No one can serve two masters; you cannot serve God and money, the same saying and its warning.
The love of money is a root of all kinds of evil, the sin of the scoffing Pharisees and the complacent rich man.
Do not harden your heart against your poor brother, the covenant command the rich man ignored at his gate.
Jesus raises a man named Lazarus, yet the leaders plot to kill Him, confirming that a resurrection will not persuade the willfully deaf.

study.oneStory: On the road to Jerusalem Jesus teaches His people to hold money as a servant spent for eternity, never as a master that enslaves and damns. The rich man and Lazarus lift the curtain on the eternal weight of how we live and give now, and the closing word, they have Moses and the Prophets, roots our hope in the sufficiency of Scripture, which points from first to last to the Christ who would soon rise from the dead, the sign a hardened world would still refuse.

quiz.q: Why does the master commend the shrewd manager?

options.text: For his decisive foresight in preparing for his future, not for his dishonesty.
options.note: Luke 16:8 praises his shrewdness while still calling him the dishonest manager.

options.text: Because he acted cleverly to secure a welcome for himself.
options.note: True to the story, but it omits that the commendation is for foresight, and the man remains 'dishonest'.

options.text: Because cheating the master turned out to be acceptable.
options.note: The dishonesty is not endorsed; only the urgent foresight is held up as an example.

options.text: Because he faithfully repaid everything he had wasted.
options.note: He did not repay; he reduced the debtors' bills to win their favor.

quiz.q: What does Jesus teach about God and money?

options.text: No one can serve two masters; you cannot serve both God and money.
options.note: Luke 16:13 makes money a rival master to God, not merely a danger.

options.text: Money is dangerous and should be handled with care.
options.note: True but weaker; Jesus says money is a competing master, not just a risk.

options.text: Wealth is always sinful and must be given away entirely.
options.note: Jesus calls for faithful stewardship and generosity, not a blanket condemnation of possessions.

options.text: You can serve God and money if you keep them balanced.
options.note: Jesus explicitly denies this; you cannot serve both.

quiz.q: What was the rich man's failure in the story of Lazarus?

options.text: He feasted in splendor while ignoring the starving Lazarus at his own gate.
options.note: Luke 16:19-21 sets his daily luxury against the beggar he stepped past.

options.text: He was rich and lived comfortably.
options.note: Wealth itself is not the charge; his callous neglect of the poor at his gate is.

options.text: He failed to give Lazarus a proper burial.
options.note: The issue is his neglect in life, not funeral arrangements.

options.text: He gave away too much and impoverished himself.
options.note: The opposite: he hoarded his splendor and ignored the needy.

quiz.q: What does the great chasm signify?

options.text: A fixed, uncrossable divide beyond death, showing eternity is settled by this life.
options.note: Luke 16:26 describes a gulf no one can cross, underlining the finality of the divide.

options.text: That the rich man and Lazarus were now far apart.
options.note: True but shallow; the point is that the separation is permanent and cannot be crossed.

options.text: A temporary barrier that mercy might later remove.
options.note: The chasm is fixed and uncrossable; it is not portrayed as temporary.

options.text: That the afterlife has no real consequences.
options.note: The chasm precisely establishes real and lasting consequences.

quiz.q: What is Abraham's final answer to the rich man's plea for his brothers?

options.text: They have Moses and the Prophets; if they will not hear them, they will not be persuaded even by a resurrection.
options.note: Luke 16:29-31 grounds faith in hearing Scripture, not in signs.

options.text: Scripture is enough to warn the brothers to repent.
options.note: True, but it omits the sharp point that even a resurrection will not persuade the deaf heart.

options.text: A miracle from the dead would surely make them believe.
options.note: Abraham says the opposite; the resistant will not believe even then.

options.text: Nothing could ever warn the brothers in time.
options.note: They can be warned; they have Moses and the Prophets to hear now.

questions: The manager saw his reckoning coming and acted at once. Are you as clear-eyed and urgent about eternity as you are about your finances and future security?
Jesus says you cannot serve both God and money. Be honest: which one is actually steering your decisions, your calendar, and your worries?
The rich man never noticed Lazarus at his gate. Who is the person God has placed at the edge of your comfortable life, and are you stepping over them?
How you handle a very little thing, money, reveals whether you can be trusted with true riches. What would faithful, generous stewardship look like for you this month?
Abraham said they have Moses and the Prophets. Do you truly believe Scripture is enough to change a heart, or are you waiting for a more spectacular sign before you obey what you already know?

translationNotes.note: The debts are given in Greek measures, baths of oil and cors of wheat, amounting to roughly hundreds of gallons of oil and tens of tons of wheat, enormous sums that show how much the manager was reducing to win the debtors' goodwill.

'Abraham's side' translates a Greek phrase, into Abraham's bosom, a Jewish image for the place of comfort among the blessed dead. 'Hades' is the realm of the dead; here it is a place of conscious torment for the rich man.

The clause rendered 'everyone is forcing his way into it' can also be translated 'everyone is urged to enter it'; either way it describes the pressing summons of the kingdom now proclaimed since John.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Luke 16:1-31. Headings, line breaks, footnote markers, and speaker labels were skipped when assembling verse text; no em or en dashes occur in this passage. Sermon status set to none; no sermon from the trusted cluster was verified this session.
